summary: The main claim is: Developers can access this new model by calling gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 in the API.
The main claim is: TLDR: We’re running a small iteration of MLAB (~10 participants) in Oxford towards the end of September.
The main claim is: I think it's likely that if AI doesn't kill everyone, developing great model evals and causing everyone to use them will be a big part of that.

Developers must be able to identify dangerous capabilities (through "dangerous capability evaluations") and the propensity of models to apply their capabilities for harm (through "alignment evaluations").
The main claim is: The problem: In scenario 1, the mesa-optimizer is still, by definition, an optimizer, so any general claims about optimization apply to it as well.
The main claim is: There will be AI transitions at different times in different domains 2.
This post is a transcript of a recorded discussion, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.

top_comment.author: Jackson Wagner
top_comment.text: It seems pretty understandable that ChatGPT didn't understand the context/detail you were looking for, since writing instructions on the level of 10-second actions is a rare and unusual situation. People notoriously find it difficult to give precise cooking instructions, to the extent that it has become a common classroom activity (my fourth grade teacher did this) to teach kids about instructional writing style: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Ct-lOOUqmyY
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: It seems pretty understandable that ChatGPT didn't understand the context/detail you were looking for, since writing instructions on the level of 10-second actions is a rare and unusual situation.
